name: Haleakala National Park
short_description: Explore volcanic gemstones at this 7,200 ft Hawaiian national park. Find obsidian and quartz specimens formed by ancient volcanic activity on the slopes of Haleakala crater.
description:
Haleakala National Park sits atop the massive Haleakala shield volcano on the island of Maui. At 7,200 feet elevation, the park's unique volcanic landscape offers rockhounders the chance to collect volcanic specimens including obsidian and quartz in a setting unlike anywhere else in the continental United States. The park's cinder cones, lava flows, and volcanic rock formations tell the story of Hawaiian island geology spanning thousands of years.

The high-elevation terrain and gentle slopes make this location accessible to most hikers, though the thin volcanic soil and sharp lava rock require careful footing and appropriate protection. The park's volcanic geology stems from the shield volcano eruptions that built the Hawaiian Islands. Obsidian pockets and quartz-bearing volcanic rocks can be found along established trails and in areas where erosion has exposed fresh volcanic material.

Visiting Haleakala requires understanding that it is a protected national park with strict collection regulations. While rockhounding opportunities exist in designated areas, all collecting must follow National Park Service rules designed to preserve the park's geological and cultural significance. The park's extreme elevation and changeable weather conditions demand proper preparation and physical fitness.
history: Haleakala, meaning 'House of the Sun' in Hawaiian, was formed by shield volcano eruptions over thousands of years. The volcano remains geologically active, and its last eruption occurred in 1790, making the exposed lava flows and volcanic features here among the youngest in Hawaii. The obsidian and quartz formations visible today crystallized from the silica-rich lava flows that erupted as the volcano built the island of Maui.
tips: Check with the National Park Service Visitor Center before collecting to confirm current regulations and designated collection areas - rules change seasonally and some zones are permanently off-limits. Focus your search along designated trails where erosion has naturally exposed volcanic rock; avoid disturbing vegetation or hiking off-trail to protect the park's fragile alpine ecosystem. Look for glassy black obsidian fragments and clear quartz crystals in areas with exposed dark volcanic rock and cinder deposits. Bring plenty of water and sun protection - the elevation and volcanic rock create intense sun reflection, and weather conditions change rapidly at this height.
terrain_notes: The park sits at 7,200 feet elevation on gentle volcanic slopes. Terrain is primarily bare volcanic rock, cinder, and sparse alpine vegetation. Trails are well-maintained but exposed to wind and weather. Walking distances from parking areas range from 0.5 to 3 miles depending on which area you access. The volcanic rock surface is sharp and uneven; sturdy closed-toe hiking boots are essential.
best_season: April - October. The high elevation makes winter months (November - March) cold and frequently cloudy, with reduced visibility and occasional snow. Summer and early fall offer the most stable weather, though afternoon clouds and wind are common year-round. The park is open daily, but road conditions and visibility are best in warmer months.
written_directions: From Kahului (Maui's main airport and nearest town), take HI-36 southeast toward Kula for approximately 15 miles. Continue on HI-37, then take HI-377 south toward the park entrance. Follow HI-378 (Crater Road) as it winds upward for approximately 20 miles to reach Haleakala National Park's entrance and summit area. The drive takes 1.5 to 2 hours from Kahului. Park entrance station is located at the top of the road.
road_conditions: Crater Road (HI-378) is a paved highway with numerous switchbacks and steep grades. Road surface is well-maintained but narrow in places. High elevation and weather can impact conditions; check road status before visiting. Standard passenger vehicles are adequate, though towing is not recommended due to steep grades.
parking_notes: Multiple parking areas available at the summit and along Crater Road. The main summit parking area has capacity for 50+ vehicles. Parking is free and available on a first-come, first-served basis. Some pullouts along Crater Road also provide limited roadside parking.
collection_rules: Haleakala National Park is a protected national park - all collection is heavily restricted. Hand collection of small specimens only is permitted in designated areas with National Park Service approval. No motorized tools, no digging or excavation of any kind. No commercial collection. Check with the visitor center for current permitted collection zones, as regulations change and many areas prohibit collection entirely. Specimens collected must be for personal use only and must remain within the park's guidelines. Violation of park collection rules can result in significant fines.
rules:
Hand collection of small specimens only in designated areas
No motorized tools or excavation
No commercial collection
Personal use only
Check with visitor center before collecting
Many park zones prohibit collection entirely
Violators subject to federal penalties
nearest_services: Kahului, approximately 30 miles away, has full services including gas, food, and water. Kula, a small mountain town approximately 15 miles from the park entrance, offers limited services.
accessibility_notes: Terrain is generally moderate with well-maintained trails, but exposure to wind and altitude makes it challenging for those unaccustomed to elevation. Volcanic rock is extremely sharp - proper footwear is mandatory. Some trails have steep drop-offs and uneven footing. The high elevation (7,200 ft) may affect visitors with respiratory issues or heart conditions. No wheelchair access on most trails. Visitors with mobility concerns should stick to paved areas near the parking lots.

faq.question: What gemstones and minerals can you find at Haleakala National Park in Hawaii?
faq.answer: Haleakala's volcanic landscape yields obsidian (glassy black volcanic glass), quartz crystals, and basalt specimens. The park's young lava flows and cinder cones contain obsidian pockets that formed as silica-rich lava cooled rapidly. Clear quartz crystals are found in volcanic rock throughout the summit area. Specimens here are unique Hawaiian volcanic gems not commonly available elsewhere.

faq.question: Is rockhounding allowed at Haleakala National Park and what are the collection rules?
faq.answer: Rockhounding is permitted but heavily restricted at Haleakala National Park. Only hand collection of small specimens is allowed in designated areas with prior approval from the visitor center. No digging, excavation, or motorized tools are permitted. No commercial collection. Many park zones prohibit collection entirely. Always check with the visitor center before attempting to collect, as regulations change seasonally.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Haleakala volcano in Maui?
faq.answer: The best rockhounding season at Haleakala is April through October. Winter months (November - March) bring cold temperatures, frequent clouds, and occasional snow at the 7,200 ft elevation, reducing visibility and access. Summer months offer the most stable weather conditions, though afternoon clouds and wind are common year-round. Always check current weather conditions before visiting.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect specimens at Haleakala National Park Hawaii?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal hand collection in designated areas, but you must check with the National Park Service Visitor Center before collecting. Park regulations are strict - hand collection only, no excavation or motorized tools allowed. Collection is prohibited in many park zones to protect geological and cultural resources. The $30 park entrance fee is required for all visitors.

faq.question: How difficult is rockhounding at Haleakala and is it suitable for beginners?
faq.answer: Haleakala is moderate difficulty, suitable for beginners in reasonable physical condition. The main challenge is the 7,200 ft elevation, which can affect visitors unaccustomed to high altitude. Well-maintained trails provide access to collecting areas. However, volcanic rock is extremely sharp and requires sturdy hiking boots. The exposure to wind and changeable weather demands proper preparation. Families with children ages 8+ can participate if properly equipped.

faq.question: Where exactly should I search for obsidian and quartz at Haleakala National Park?
faq.answer: Focus your search along designated trails where natural erosion has exposed volcanic rock formations and cinder deposits. The summit area and Crater Road pullouts provide access to exposed lava flows where obsidian fragments and quartz crystals are visible. Always stay on marked trails and collect only from designated areas approved by the visitor center. Avoid disturbing vegetation or hiking off-trail, as the park's alpine ecosystem is fragile and protected.

meta_description: Find obsidian and quartz at Haleakala volcano in Hawaii. Hand collection allowed in designated areas. $30 entry fee. Check rules before visiting.
